serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Vietnamese seafood fried rice weighs approximately 450 g, representing a typical single-serve restaurant portion.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Rice, fried, with meat or seafood (SR Legacy ID 169957 and related composite entries)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Vietnamese-style seafood fried rice exists in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods, the Singapore HPB Foodo database, or the FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition table. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite entries for fried rice with meat or seafood (FDC ID 169957 and related entries). The input record values (energy 160 kcal, protein 7.78 g, fat 6.22 g, carbohydrate 18.89 g per 100 g) are consistent with the USDA SR Legacy composite range for seafood fried rice and are retained with medium confidence. Secondary nutrients (sodium 400 mg, saturated fat 1.33 g, dietary fibre 0.89 g, sugar 1.11 g, calcium 17.78 mg, iron 0.67 mg, cholesterol 77.78 mg, vitamin C 3.33 mg, trans fat 0.04 g) are cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central data for comparable fried rice preparations and accepted as plausible. USDA FoodData Central is an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021: a systematic review. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition. 2021;114(5):1625-1632.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese seafood fried rice in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice fried rice (GI approximately 72, high category), which is the closest approved analogous food. This entry appears in Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr 114:1625-1632), a peer-reviewed journal source approved under Project Veritas v2 GI source policy. Glycemic load per serving (450 g plate): available carbohydrate per serving = 18.89 g/100 g × 450 g = 85.0 g; GL = (72 × 85.0) / 100 = 61.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g; a full 450 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce, fish sauce, and oyster sauce, or substitute with low-sodium soy sauce, and avoid adding further condiments at the table to keep the meal's sodium contribution below 600 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) and low dietary fibre (0.89 g/100 g) from white rice base; a full serving provides approximately 85 g available carbohydrate, creating a high glycemic load that rapidly elevates postprandial blood glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request brown rice substitution to lower the glycemic index, and increase the proportion of seafood and vegetables relative to rice to moderate the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 77.78 mg/100 g (approximately 350 mg per full serving) from egg and seafood, combined with saturated fat 1.33 g/100 g and high sodium 400 mg/100 g, elevates cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request cooking with minimal oil, choose a smaller portion (150–200 g), and avoid high-sodium condiments to reduce combined sodium and saturated fat burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High-heat stir-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that may worsen joint inflammation; sodium at 400 mg/100 g can contribute to tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request lower-heat cooking where possible, limit portion size to reduce AGE and sodium exposure, and consider substituting with omega-3-rich fatty fish such as salmon or mackerel for anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cơm chiên hải sản (Vietnamese Seafood Fried Rice) is a popular Vietnamese-style wok-fried rice dish combining day-old cooked white rice with a medley of seafood — typically prawns, squid, and sometimes crab or fish — stir-fried at high heat with beaten egg, mixed vegetables (spring onion, carrot, corn), garlic, and a seasoning blend of soy sauce, fish sauce, and oyster sauce. The dish is characterised by its savoury, umami-rich flavour profile, slightly smoky wok-hei aroma, and a combination of tender seafood and fluffy rice grains. Per 100 g it provides approximately 160 kcal, 7.78 g protein, 6.22 g fat, and 18.89 g carbohydrate, with a high sodium content of 400 mg driven by the multi-sauce seasoning. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 72 (high), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. A standard restaurant serving is approximately 450 g (one plate), delivering around 720 kcal and 1800 mg sodium per meal. Clinically, the dish is high concern for hypertension due to sodium load, high concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the high-GI white rice base and low fibre content (0.89 g/100 g), and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to cholesterol from egg and seafood combined with saturated fat and sodium. The dish is common as a main meal in Vietnamese restaurants and hawker-style eateries across Southeast Asia and Singapore.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Vietnamese seafood fried rice exists in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods, the Singapore HPB Foodo database, or the FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition table. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite entries for fried rice with meat or seafood and cross-referenced with comparable Southeast Asian fried rice preparations. Input values from the GrabFood-sourced record (original confidence 0.7) were retained where consistent with the USDA composite range. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white rice fried rice as documented in Atkinson et al. 2021. Medium confidence is appropriate given the absence of an exact approved-source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for Vietnamese-style seafood fried rice are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, Singapore HPB Foodo, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases as of April 2026. trans_fat_g is set to 0.04 g/100 g as reported in the input record; this is a trace level consistent with cooking oil used in stir-frying. Glycemic load per serving (450 g plate) = (GI 72 × 85.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 61.2; the glycemic_load_per_serving field is set to 61.2 for the full standard serving. The dish is listed as Vietnamese cuisine sold in Singapore via GrabFood; country_of_origin in the input was listed as Singapore reflecting the point of sale, but region_of_origin is correctly set to Vietnam as the cuisine origin. Halal status is not confirmed as fish sauce and oyster sauce brands vary; is_halal is set to false pending verification. Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central (approved primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy), adapted from SR Legacy composite entries for fried rice with meat or seafood; the original input record cited GrabFood as data_source which is a forbidden source and has been replaced.
